speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Jenny Halverson Driscoll took place at her home in Moscow on February 17th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: My father came from Norway and he landed in Astoria, Oregon. He landed in Chicago because he lived at the boarding house there and there was a very best grandmother. They were married in Chicago.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He loved to fish as a fisherman. He heard that there were homesteads in Idaho. They came out to Astoria, Oregon first. He stayed there and fished.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We worked on the Columbia River as a fisherman. There was a pretty good sized Norwegian community in Astoria.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: My mother was so afraid that he would drown because they knew that so many fishermen had drowned. It was common. People were drowning when they were fishing in those days. They were just in boats, you know, just in rowboats and fishing. He made a good living fishing. Then they heard about the homesteads up north that were being opened up. My father decided that they would go up and get a homestead so my mother was happy about that. They came up to Genesee country and everything was taken. There was one man, Mother Mary Peterson, that was dissatisfied and he sold his rights to my father. He sold 160 acres for $700. A wonderful 160. It was just a cabin. Oh, my mother was so happy with it because she got away from the water. They had no money to do anything with. He had enough money, he bought a plow and a team of horses. A walking plow. I can remember when part of that farm wasn't even under cultivation. It was just hard.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: She was going to have a baby. Oh, she wanted a bedroom so bad. I leaned onto the shack of the house. My father said, well, we don't have any money. We can't build any bedroom out here because we don't have any money. Pretty soon she said, well, I have a gold watch and chain and you have a gold watch and chain. Maybe we could trade that for some lumber. So, okay, if you're willing to do that, I'm willing. He said, when we get to be able, I'll buy you the nicest watch I can find. So, he went up north of Troy. I think it was the Erickson saw mill, I'm sure as well. And traded their watches for lumber. Well, anyway, they got the lumber and built the lean -to to the house. It's still there on that house on my published farm. But it didn't get it finished the time the baby came. And while she was in bed, came a big snowstorm and a blizzard and a drip of snow on her bed. But they lived through it and they were happy because they got a bedroom.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he built the lean -to or was that there when he got there?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, he built it. There was no building on the... There was a cabin on there, just a one room house, a cabin.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And they built the lean -to. And that's still there on that house. They've been building to it, adding to it. And we were all, six of us were born in that bedroom. And then when I was about eight years old, I think I must have been about seven or eight years old, my father went to Spokane and he brought her home a beautiful whole watch. And I can remember so well, she was so happy she cried over it, over this gold watch and chain that he brought her. And I've had it up to, just up to last year and I was afraid somebody might pick it up and say, it's a beautiful watch. And I gave it to my daughter to keep. I gave it to her. So I don't have it, but I've had it for years. My father gave it to me when I was confirmed. He gave me that watch and chain for me to wear. But I kept it all the time, but I was always just, I moved around from apartment to the farm and around and I was afraid that somebody might pick it up, you know. So I gave it to my daughter and she's got it.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That was one story about my whole good life. So by then, by that time, he had done well enough that he could afford to... Oh, he had a couple, three crops, you see.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And all of that place used to produce 60 wishles to the acre. That place is still there. It's only been in two hands. My father's and a Peloponnese burger bought it. It's still there, six miles north of Genesee. Well, he bought another 160 acres across the road from another homesteader. By the name of Daggett, George Daggett. And he bought that and he had 320 acres of land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where just was this? What creek was it on? Creek? Yeah, was it near Cow Creek?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, it was about five miles from Cow Creek. Cow Creek is where all the Norwegians... There's a regular Norwegian settlement, Norwegian Lutherans settled down along both sides of the creek, you might say. And my father organized the Lutheran Church down there. My father was one of the charter members of that church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There were two, weren't there?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: There was two Lutheran churches just a half a mile apart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which one did he belong to?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: The one that's on the cemetery. But then they consolidate after a few years.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, he didn't live close to them though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was four or five miles away?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yes, from Cow Creek. He was between Linville and Cow Creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that require him and the family to travel over there to get to go to church on Sundays?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We used to go in a lumber wagon.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I can remember, it was a little girl. And then he was the first man to have a hack. First man to have a hack. And it was the only hack, only vehicle they could carry a casket in. And everyone, for every funeral, carried one. He drove a... he was the first in this hack. But we had a two -seated hack. Before he got that, we used to go in the big old lumber wagon. Oh, I can remember so many things. But we were happy as hats could be. All of us. Neighbours and all of us. I used to walk three miles to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: You know, I sit here and I watch those kids climbing in and out of the buses. And I think, how lucky they are. I wonder if they understand and realize how lucky they are. Here I was, only six years old, and I walked three miles to school in the morning and three miles back home and carried my lunch pail. We all did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that hard? Wasn't that awfully hard?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I mean, three miles is a long way. No, that didn't bother me one bit.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I guess that's why I'm living to be eight years old. Because I didn't baby myself. We had a lot of fun. We had neighbours that lived a mile beyond us. And those kids walked to our place and we all went to school. They walked four miles to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You kids walked in a group? You went together?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We went in groups and we went by ourselves each other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long do you think it took you to get to the school?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We left home at eight o 'clock and got there by nine.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And played a ton to play a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you play on the way? You played on the way?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, we didn't play on the way. We just walked. And the school teacher used to board with my mother at our home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lady or a man?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Her name was a lady teacher. I forget what her name was. I can't spell her name. I have a picture of the school. I was ten years old when I was in that picture of my brothers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it very large? The number of kids?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, I imagine we were around twenty, twenty -five.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Eight grades. Eight grades. From first grade to the eighth.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, that's nice.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, I don't know. I can remember. They had all the food, you know. They had the thrashing crews. They used to thrash for the headed stacks. They had a header and they had these long headed stacks. And in ninety -three, it started raining the fifth of September. And it never let up all winter. And everybody lost their crop. And some of those four homesteaders had their places mortgaged and they foreclosed on our grocery bill. And I can remember my mother, of course, she churned butter and she raised chickens and she had eggs and we had beef. We raised everything we ate, you know. We didn't suffer for anything to eat. And she'd buy green coffee beans and she'd roast them and then she'd take half barley and roast barley with the coffee. And that made nice brown coffee. I can remember that part of it.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But what about not having... Your father must have lost his crop too that year like everybody else. He lost his crop. He lost it, a big crop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of shape did that leave him in?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, I don't think it left him.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I didn't manage it. It didn't leave him in too bad a shape. But in the spring, there was about two feet of spoiled grain. And he... I can remember them pitching that all off on the top of the stack. And they thrashed them right in the middle of the stack. They saved that. And that was taxes and for money for different things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let's see, how old? You're 88 now?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I will be 88 in June.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what year were you born in? What year would that be that you were born in?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were five years old then?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I just don't remember all of it. I remember it all. I remember the rain and how they go to church and pray for rain and all. I know one Sunday morning... It's funny, you know, some things like that stick in a kid's mind. And I felt the sorrow for my father because my mother said, Now, John, today there's an extra offering. We have to give a little extra money for the church so we can keep our minister. And he put his hand in his pocket and he took a, I've got ten cents, I've got all I've got. And I can remember, oh, I felt so sorry for my father thinking, Oh, they had ten cents. That's all the money he had. So, what she says, give it. My mother said, Well, John, give it. Oh, I tell you, I can remember hard times. And you know, now, when I think about the way the young couple, the women demand, oh boy, do they demand. The poor husband, no wonder they can't get along. The prices are high, but still the women demand. They must have. They must have every electric appliance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did your mother get by then? How did your mother do then? I mean, did she get by without much?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We had an old, we had a stove, just an old fashioned stove, cook stove. And we had a small heating stove in the living room. It kept us warm. And she churned butter, she sold butter for ten cents a pound. She sold eggs for ten cents a dozen. And she, and she, and my father raised, always raised a beef, a butchered. And they made sausage out of that, and they made

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: dried beef they used to make. And, oh, we had plenty to eat, plenty of chicken and pasta. We had good food. My mother baked all her own bread.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How busy was she kept working? How busy was she kept working? Was she working all the time?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: She got to work all day. She sat down in the afternoon crocheting. Did all our sewing, even to the shirts for the boys. But she died when she was forty -two, just a young woman. And we had, unless the six of us, I was not quite twelve. And as little boys, it wasn't quite two. And I finished the eighth grade, that's as far as I could go. And my grandmother lived with us for eighteen years, my father's mother. And she was seventy -three, and she lived with us. And between grandmother and I, we did the housework after mother died.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And she lived, she died in nineteen -seven, she died, and then I did it all. I raised my little brother, I took all care of him from the time he was two until he was a man.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I dressed him, took him to school, took all care of him. Now the poor fella sitting in a wheelchair in Spokane had a paralyzed stroke. And I had a brother sixteen when my mother died, and fourteen, and ten, and I was twelve. So you see, we were quite a family. So my father had quite a struggle. But he was a good manager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did your mother die?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well she had a miscarriage, a doctor didn't know his business, and she got an infection and died. She wouldn't have died at this day and age.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That's what happened to her. But you think it was needless, if she had been cared for better, it wouldn't have happened. Oh, never would have happened. Well is that because, it's not just because she had the delivery, just because it happened at home.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well it happened at home, and we had a doctor in Genesee, and my brother had to go horseback to Genesee and get him, and he came horseback up there to care of her, but he didn't know his business. And she got an infection and died in a week.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But we always had, my father was a good provider, always had a nice home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what? It must have been, I know another man I was talking to just recently told me that when his wife got sick, when they were first married, and they had all the young kids, he was scared, just really scared that if she died, what would happen?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: All the kids and all the work.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah, well my father was worried too, I'll tell you, but he stuck with it. Then he bought this, he paid $100 for the home place, and he bought the place across the road, just across the road for $4 ,000, $160. Okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother have a midwife there as well as the doctor?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh yeah, she had a neighbor lady.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That was a pity.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you finish, you finished the 8th grade, but could you have gone to school? Is there any other place?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I was going to go to school, I was going to Tacoma, to boarding school, all the girls in our church went to Tacoma, there was an academy there, Pacific Lutheran Academy. I was going there, and I had my trunk packed, and I was going to go and my grandmother cried both day and night because I was leaving, and I said, I won't leave your grandma, I'll pack my trunk and stay home. And I shed a good many tears because I couldn't go, but I loved my grandmother, so I didn't, we didn't send our old people to the old folks' home at that time. We took care of our own. That's what I think is so awful now. Old, the young people, they could just as well take care of some of those old people in their home. That's true.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They do, why send them out there and take all their earnings, everything they ever made?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That's why I think these times are awful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your grandmother still able to do very much at the time she came?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, yeah, she took care of all the milk, and she made, she churned the butter, and she carried the cream, and oh, she did a lot of things. So she was really active then, there was no... Well, she was active, and she died at 80. Seven years ago, in 1970, I must say. After you.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: It was after you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah, she stayed for a good number of years after...

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Seven years after my mother died. My mother died in 1900.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the Pacific Lutheran Academy like? What was that school like?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, I don't know, I can't remember, I couldn't tell you, I never went down, I didn't go down because... Oh, I see, you wouldn't have started there until... I went home, I was going to go, but my grandmother, she felt so bad about me leaving home that she cried and said she couldn't get along without me, so I stayed home. But I've been blessed anyway. She died, her last words were asking God to bless me, and He's blessed me. He really has blessed me in many ways, I have a wonderful husband but I didn't get to keep him long enough. And his folks are homesteaded on Driscoll Ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you heard of that place?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, sure. Uh -huh. His father was the first one in there. My husband's father.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that John?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, that was George Driscoll. And then his father came, then he went, after he got out, first he went to Fix Ridge, and over there everything was taken. And Mr. Fix told him, across the canyon, I don't think there's been a soul over there. He said, I think there's land to be taken there. So he went over across the canyon over there, no one had been there, to Homestead. So he had five brothers and two sisters, and he went over there and located the homestead for each one of them. And went back to Canada, to New Brunswick, Canada, and brought the whole outfit out here. His brothers and his two sisters. His one sister was Mrs. Cunningham and the other one was Mrs. Dunn. They brought them all out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were all ready to come?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, they were all anxious to come.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Were they born Canadians or had they come from... They were born Canadians.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But some of their ancestors came from Ireland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were at home and having to work to keep the family going, was it pretty tough to raise a bunch of kids at your age?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Because you were only 13 yourself.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, I didn't mind it. We all got along beautifully. We all worked together.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: The boys did all the outside work and I never worked outdoors. My father would never let me work outdoors. I did the housework. I didn't mind it at all. And my grandmother was the help of my grandmother, you see.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I stayed home and did the work until I was married. I was 28 when I was married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the work that the women did cooking for threshing?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: You mentioned that. Your mother did that. What was the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Menu?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah, the way it went. Oh, my men. They were good cooks. My goodness, they made their own bread and biscuits and roast and mashed potatoes and brown gravy and sliced beef. It was just much better than they do now. A lot of fruit. We had all kinds of fruit. All kinds of berries in our place. They had good food, don't worry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work as a cook ever yourself?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I cooked all... I mean on the threshing. Oh, no.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: After I got old enough to do that, they had cookwagons. Cookshacks. The heart of the men that owned the threshing machine had a cook wagon right along with the... When I got old enough to, you see. That's when my mother was living and I was just a little girl. Then they had to cook for the threshers. As the farmers prospered along and got more land broke out and raised more wheat, you see. Then the man that owned the threshing machine, he had a covered wagon and a couple of girls had cooked for the threshers. And they had a rouge about to go to town for their groceries, but I never did work for them. I used to visit them a lot. All I did was entirely different living what we're living now. They had a cook wagon. Then you see... Then they had a water... a pillow that hauled the water.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Bundle wagons, you know, hauling the bundles. Of course, after 93, they quit the headers. Everybody quit the headers because they could... When they bought binders and bound the grain and shocked it, see. It didn't have to be dead ripe. They cut the grain in shocks and it ripened in the shocks and the rain came.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Those bundles would dry out, you see, in those shocks. That's why they switched from... Binders to headers. From headers to binders. I didn't know that was why they had done that. I knew they had switched, but I didn't know why. That happened after 93.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: You see, in 93, all the farmers lost their crops. The rain. And then the binders came in and they bound them in shocks. And then they would stack their grain and they stacked it in a way that... If it rained, it shed off. The rain wouldn't soak in, see. But those header stacks, they soaked right into them, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many neighbors of yours, can you remember, did many neighbors leave after 93 because many people did lose their land?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, I can't remember. I know they did, but I can't remember. I don't remember who they'd be now. I wonder who they lost it to. I heard that Vollmer and the... J .P. Vollmer, you bet your life. He foreclosed on them for groceries. He had a grocery store in Genesee. And then one year, and before that, if all the people lost their grain and the warehouse burned. I can remember, I was just a little girl, I guess. I must have been about 7 or 8, 8 or 8, maybe 8 or 9. And my father had a whole whale of a crop of wheat. And he didn't think he could get it into town fast enough, so he bought a new wagon so he could haul his wheat and get his wheat into the warehouse. But instead of... the farmers hauled the wheat into the woods warehouse, and they gave them the receipt on the warehouse, and they had them pile the wheat on the boxcar and ship it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they had the receipt on the warehouse, don't you see? And coming home from town one day, my mother said to my father, John, did you insure the wheat today?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, it isn't going to burn. That night it burned. He lost everything of his crop in the warehouse. But his wheat didn't burn because it had been shipped to Portland on that boxcar. He had his hired man put him on the boxcar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get money for his wheat then?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Nothing.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He had nothing to show for that he had receipts, but they were on the warehouse, the warehouse burned. Oh, that was another crook, I tell you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that walmer too?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, that was woods. Gee, I think it was woods.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But they had a history of Idaho printed years ago. And they had John P. Walmer, of course he made a lot of money. His picture on the front page of the book, and he said, well that's a fine book, my father said. There was a description of every farmer in there, told a history of him. And my father bought the book, but he told John P. Walmer's first leaf out, burned it up. He said, he isn't worth being in there.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, I know that, of course I'm sure you know the story, how Troy was originally named Walmer and the people changed the name. Because they didn't like him.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I remember the Rosenstein. Oh, he used to trade with him all the time. Mr. Rosenstein, old Utah Nigel fellow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a guy was he? What kind of a guy was he?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I've heard some of that. He was a Jew, and had an awfully nice wife. They used to have dry goods and groceries.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They had three or four girls. They didn't have any boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard that, did you hear that he was, did you think that he was a fair merchant?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, he was wonderful.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He was wonderful. I never went into that store unless I got a sack of candy. He was so good to all the kids, and he was wonderful. And when you bought your groceries and had them all packed, always a sack of candy. But that was the only merchant in town. And then the next store, then the Pollocks came in. And they had a store, the Pollocks.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But we never gave up Rosenstein.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He was a nice little fellow.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: His wife was probably Jewish too then. Oh yes, she was Jewish.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But she was, oh she was so good to me. I remember after my mother died, she was so good to me. She'd always give me something, you know.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But then there was people that didn't like the Rosensteins too. As far as I can remember, we all liked that store. And I felt so bad when they left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they retiring when they left?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, they went to San Francisco. I think that's where they went to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So after your mother died, what did she do that you appreciated so much?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, I don't know. I can't just remember, but she was always doing nice things for me. She'd give me a nice handkerchief or she'd give me something all the time. And Mr. Rosenstein, he gave us a good many sacks of candy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that store have all the goods and merchandise that a family would need?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh yes, everything. Clothing and everything.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah, they had everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to go to town? Did you get to go to Genesee very much?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, plan. I rode horseback to Genesee all the time. I used to go down, my father... I wasn't even long before my mother died. I used to ride horseback into town to get the mail. I had a little white pony called Fanny. And it was an Indian pony. My dad bought it from the Indians. Nesperous Indians. And I'd meet just droves of Indians on my way to Genesee. They were traveling from Nesperous to Cowellville reservation. They traveled back and forth from Nesperous to Cowellville. And I used to be so afraid of them. Oh, I used to be so afraid of those Indians. And my horse is an Indian pony. He had bought it from the Indians and she'd want to go with the Indians, you know. And I used to have an awful time to get her to go through that row of Indians. And I can still see those old squaws just laughing. Oh my, we used to just laugh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would happen? The horse would try to turn in with the pack? Would the horse try to turn in with the other Cayuses?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yes. Uh -huh.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: She didn't want to be alone, I guess. She'd been with those Indians before. I may get off the road. They'd come up by our place. And we had a pump right outside the back door. They'd pump water and drink water and talk and chatter, get on the horses and go. They never did any harm. They'd all get off the road, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to know any Indians or speak to them in those days?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They'd speak to me. Hello. Not that I was scared. Not scared of the Indians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you scared?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I rode down to town to get my father's. He took a Norwegian paper called the Scandinavian from Chicago. And he had to have that. It was published every, it was coming to Genesee every Wednesday and every Saturday. And the Wednesdays when I'd go down on horseback and get it, get the paper, get the mail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you usually have any other mail besides the newspaper?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, he'd have letters from all these people, you know.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Norway and in the east there he had a lot of bread. All of this in the east. Oh, he had a lot of letters.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: So quite a few of the family, quite a few of his family left Norway and came to America.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, all his family. His father was a ship builder in Norway. He built freight boats, freighters and sailing boats.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And after he died, they all came to America. The mother in all. And she lived with her sons in Dakota. They hosted in Dakota and Minnesota. But my father, he came out to Idaho. And the host said, well he went to Chicago first and he sailed, he worked on the Great Lakes. He loved the water. But my mother was afraid of it. But he drowned. So that's why she wanted him to go to the homestead, fly and get a homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he done any farming before he lived here?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, he hadn't done a bit of farming until he came to his own farm.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He must have had to learn quite a bit, fast.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He had to learn by himself. He bought a plow and horses and he didn't have a gang plow. He had a walking plow. I can remember when a part of the farm was in SOD.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Natural State of Affairs. SOD. It was our pasture. We called it our pasture. And we had, he raised quite a few cattle. But he had never farmed. His father was not a farmer. He was a ship spinner, you know.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: So you figure that the reason he turned to farming was because of his wife. Yeah. She was the one.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And he was never sorry. He said he was always thankful that she got him off the water. He made more money off farming than fishing.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: You know, I have heard that, and I understand, that the farmland is better, that the Palouse farmland there, just, you know, people did well much more quickly than they did, let's say, on a lot of the ridges or as you go east, you know, towards Deery, further east you go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that true? Did your father prosper pretty early on the farm?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, yes, because, you know, you take out there in the Troy area on those ridges, they had a lot of timber to cut down and thrub out before they could start farming.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But that's a wonderful, productive country now. My son is farming on his, he's farming the great grandfather's homestead right now.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I don't know, but it seemed like my father did, he always made money on his farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he build a new house before too many years, after that first one with the lean -to?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, he didn't.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He built a living room onto the first room that was built there, that shack, as I said. Well, he built a living room out of that, an extra bedroom and two bedrooms upstairs. And we had that, we lived, and then after my mother died, it crossed the road, the place you bought had an awfully nice house on it, a real modern nice house. And we moved over there, and that's where my mother died. We were all so lonesome over there, and we didn't like to live there. So my father tore that house down and brought it across the road and added it to his original house, and we had a real nice house. Big living room, a big dining room, and a big kitchen, three bedrooms downstairs, and four bedrooms upstairs, great big house.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: So he tore it down and he rebuilt it. Uh -huh, yep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were lonely at that house?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah, my mother was so proud of that house. Of course, just across the road from us, you know, and we'd look at that house, and we wanted to go back to the old house. We didn't want to live there. So he tore the house down, had a carpenter come, tear it all down, and rebuilt it. Took the same lumber and built it onto the little house that we wanted to live.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were lonely because your mother had died there?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yes, yep.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We were all lonely over there. We didn't like it because she had lived there only one year, and she was so happy with that house and things she died, you see. We didn't want to live there either. Which was foolish too because it was a nice looking house.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, your father was pretty understanding if he was willing to move it for you. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He was a good father. Wonderful.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We had a woman working for us two years after my mother died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Charlie Jelliberg's sister?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, no. She had a place over on Nesper's, and she had a place over there. She had proved up on us. She came and lived with us for two years. But she's dead a long time ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she do the housekeeping?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yes, she did the housekeeping.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that why you were still in school?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That's why I could go to school. I got through the eighth grade, see.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, I wonder what it would be like to have to become a mother to a whole bunch of kids at such a young age. It seems like lots of responsibility for a young person to have to shoulder. I did have.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I tell you, I had a lot of responsibility, but I loved them all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The kids would mind you very good? Were the kids mind you very good, or were they pretty independent?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They'd mind me because they didn't dare do otherwise because if I told my father, they'd get it. Our grandmother lived with us. That's one thing I'll always remember about my father. He was so good to his mother. We didn't, either one of the boys or anybody said a sassy word to Grandmother. They'd get it from the neck, I'll tell you. So he was a strict father, but he was a good father. I can think of him now. He was a good father. I used to think he was awful strict and didn't like it, but he was a wonderful father.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: All those days were, people were much happier in those days than they are now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think so?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, they were satisfied. All the youngsters, they didn't care whether they had any money or not. We had fun without money. Now the kids have got money and nice clothes. I tell you, they're different. There isn't the happiness in the world today than it was at that time.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Everyone, no matter where you go, what home you go into, it seems like it's a happy home.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I used to go and stay all night for different ones and they'd come and stay all night with me, girls from school. But every home was just as happy as mine. Nobody was dissatisfied.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But now, the kids require so much. It's just like watching the kids here, school buses coming and going. I think, my land. I know some of them don't, they live around town here someplace. But still, they have to have a school bus to ride on. Of course, the country school made a difference, you see, too. There was three or four different, some country school was in an area of ten miles in the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which school did you go to?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I went to the Aurora.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Just a little school hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any of your teachers very well?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Dorothy Weather was my teacher, one of them. She did not, but she was an old little teacher. They were pioneers here in Moscow, the folks.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And then there was Clara Weather that taught one of our schools. Not our school, but it's the neighboring school.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: You say it was easy to have fun, and I'm wondering what kind of fun, what you would call fun in those days when you were young.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We played football, we played baseball, and run sheep run, and all kinds of games like that at recess and noon. We'd all eat our lunch together and then we'd start playing games. And then the school bell would ring and we'd all go in school.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, my father bought a house here in town. We used to come in here in the wintertime after we got older. The younger young people just went to school here in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time of the year would you leave the home place?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We'd leave as soon as the harvest was over and the fall work was done. And those days, as I remember, they didn't put in fall grain. It was mostly spring grain. They didn't raise fall wheat. It was spring wheat. They used a summer fall, half of it every year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Half of it?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Half of the farm used to be summer fallow, then put into wheat for the next year. They don't do that anymore. They farm differently, see. They used to work that summer fallow all summer long. There was no weed to be found.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That's because it was summer fallow. Yes, uh -huh.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That's why he always raised about sixty bushels for better.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I wonder why the summer fallow helped keep the weeds down so well.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, I don't know. They just pulled them out so they didn't grow, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He raised sixty bushels regularly? I mean, he could count on it?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Between fifty and sixty bushels.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That's terrific. Well, it was. Of course, I'll say that it's a wonderful place. He had a wonderful farm, too. Land laid just wonderful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was that? You mean, especially good exposure?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, it didn't have too steep a hills, you see.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: After my father died, we sold it. My brother raised it for a while, and they didn't like it. His wife didn't like the farming, so we sold it. We sold it for a hundred and thirty -five dollars an acre. And divided it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do during the winter in town here? What did your father do during the winters when he lived in town?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, he didn't do much of anything but sit in the house and read his paper.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And took care of the cattle and raised hogs. I see. He did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were living in town here, though, in the winters, when you were living in Moscow, what would you do? Did you ever go over to the university or anything?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, no. I stayed home and kept house to carry my kids. I went to sewing classes and learned to sew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sewing classes?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Uh -huh. I learned to dress in the dressmaking shop. I worked in the dressmaking shop to learn sewing.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And when my own daughters, when I had my own daughters, I dressed the two of them the price of one. I sold, I made all their formals and all their clothes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who taught dressmaking in Moscow here?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, they had dressmaking shops. People would bring their material to the dressmaker and she'd make, they hadn't already made clothes in those days.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They had dressmakers and they would make, the women would make their own clothes or they'd take, if you wanted something extra nice, they'd take it to the dressmaker.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Calico, five cents a yard.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That was their everyday dresses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you learn? How did you learn to do that? Did you sign up for, did you go to work for the dressmaker? How did it work?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, I just went there to learn to sew and they taught me.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And they had a fair wallet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to pay it to?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And they didn't, and they drafted their own patterns. And I learned how to draft a pattern. And then after you got your pattern drafted, you could fit in all the frills you wanted on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you pay, did you have to pay to learn?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, no.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Just a prettice, not just a prettice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So in other words, you worked and helped while you were learning?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah, uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did the average person have dresses made or was that just kind of more well -to -do people who had the shops make their dresses?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, I think the most, the average people, not very many of them, had hired dressmakers. The better class would have their dresses made, you know. I just can't remember all of it, but, well, some of the, some of the farmer's wives would have their dresses made by dressmakers. But they were everyday dresses, they made themselves.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And like I say, calicoa, five cents a yard, that was what they, addressed didn't cost very much in those days. That was their everyday dresses, but the little better dresses, you see, better material, would be ten cents a yard or maybe twenty cents a yard or something like that.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Where did you meet Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Griskell?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, well, I grew up with him. He was a cousin of a lady that, our neighbor lady, you see, and he used to come there to visit and I used to be there, a good friend of them. That's where I met him. Oh, I knew him, packed it up. His family was just a young boy, a little boy. Yeah, we grew up together. He did about the same thing as I did. He stayed home and took care of his family. And his twin, he had a twin brother that was identical. They were identical twins. He went to college and graduated up here at college and my husband stayed home, took care of the farm and took care of the rest of them. You see. So we just about did the same thing. I took care of my family, he took care of his family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that why both of you got married kind of late?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I don't know. No, I never thought about that. I went for the love of a lot of people. But we got married and we had one happy marriage. He never spoke a cross word to me and I never did him. We were the happiest we could possibly be. He was a wonderful man. He was county commissioner here in this county for eight years. He was a smart fellow.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He was a beautiful guy. Wonderful guy. But he died too young. I was talking to a man from Kendrick the other day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, you're Walt Frisco's wife, aren't you?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And I said, yes, I am. And he said, you know, he had one fault.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, what was that?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He died too young. Well, I said he couldn't help that poor fellow. He had heart trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was he county commissioner? At what period of time was he county commissioner?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, my Lord, I can't tell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it before the Second World War?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, yes. Oh, my, yes. It was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it during the Depression?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah, during the Depression. I know, I know. It was during the Depression he was county commissioner.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: So he must have taken quite an interest in government and in the way the county was rung. Oh, he was. He loved politics.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Great Democrats.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, then, he probably, probably when the Depression came and the Democrats were, and Roosevelt got in, that probably made it better for the Democrats locally.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, you know, they got the, I forget now, the farmers got something. But it had been for the President, I think that was when Roosevelt, they lost their farms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it the Agricultural Adjustment Act?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I heard about that. Yeah, I get that. And there was price support guaranteed for the crops. Yes, uh -huh.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah. But that Depression was bad, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he know Paul Carlson pretty well? Paul Carlson?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, yeah. Because he was another good Democrat. Paul Carlson was a good Democrat, too. He was a good friend of my husband.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And his son was a good friend of mine.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: When he was a county commissioner, did you, did you have much social that you had to do, like get together, have people over a lot, do political kinds of... No.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No. No. No.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We're just little normal life, just like all of us.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Of course, we had to hire a man always to take his place on the farm. Because he was gone an awful lot. He was game warden, too, for a couple of years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like to make the move over to, you moved over to Driscoll Ridge, right? What was that like, the change of going from where you grew up to over there?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, well, it wasn't too much of a change. Of course, I've been over there a lot. It wasn't much of a change. Of course, we built our home, a new home, and a barn, and dug a well, and all in one year.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And I still love to go out there. I've been going out there every summer, excepting, I don't know if my kids love to go out this summer or not. But I used to get a hired girl and go out there in the spring and stay at LaFol. I just, I love it out there. I love it on LaFol.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have dealings with the Bank of Troy ever in those earlier years?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Always. We always had dealings with the Troy Bank. The Troy Bank was the only bank that stood up under the Depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the Troy Bank like? Do you remember what it was like to deal with them in those years?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I've heard they were really good to local people. Oh, they were awfully good. They were wonderful. Frank Brocke was one of the finest men in the United States. I think everybody talked a little bit about him. And Olibowin the same way.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They helped the farmers, I'll tell you. Wonderful.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I want to ask you a couple more things about the early years around Genesee. I wanted to ask you about church and what church was like then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go regularly on Sundays?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh no, we couldn't. That's 16 miles you see, the church. We didn't go very often. But we did go to church. Of course, I was a Lutheran and my husband was a Catholic. But I raised his family Catholic and I was married 20 years to him.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And I thought, well why do I stay home and cook dinner and let them go to church?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: So I went to church too.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And that made my husband happy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father mind that?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, my father was gone. But he was very opposed to my marriage. A Catholic, but he said he was a fine man, but he said he didn't like his religion. And I said, well I don't have to be a Catholic, you know, if I don't want to. Well you held out for quite a few years anyway. But I was happy. I went with them once in a while and I raised my kids, all my poor youngsters, Catholic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what about the church when you were a kid, the Lutheran church? Did you go there on Sundays?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, my father and mother wouldn't miss church.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We never missed church on Sunday. We'd go to church on Sunday.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I was baptized and confirmed in the Lutheran church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know why they had two different churches so close?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well there was some little clause. It didn't amount to anything they claimed. I don't know where the prayer was, but anyway they got that straightened out and then they consolidated. One was Missouri, called the Missouri Lutheran, and the other one was our Savior's Lutheran. But that Missouri church, no, I don't think I ever hear of it anymore.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I don't know. Well you say there was a little clause. Well there was some little clause.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then it was an interpretation of the scripture?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: In the scripture I guess, I don't know. Okay, I just want to tell you now, one fellow told me from who grew up down there that he had heard that it was over the scriptural verse that went, Many are called but few are chosen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that sound right to you?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Many are called but few are chosen.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I know that this day and age is different from when I was young and grew up.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I was going to ask you about your neighbors. I was going to ask you about your neighbors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were growing up there, did you get together with them very much? Did you have much to do with them?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh yes, my goodness my lord. Of course we had a, Johnny McGee, he was Irish, he was one of our nearest neighbors. My father and John McGee were the best of friends. Oh they were just wonderful friends. And then all of our neighbors we were good friends with, we used to have dinners together and the men used to exchange work, you know, just good friends.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They weren't that independent that there is now.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: One neighbor was ready to help the other one, she needed it. And that was them.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Anybody was sick by the, I know my mother used to go and help out anybody that was sick, you know, she'd get off, she'd go and help her neighbor.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They don't do that now. No ma 'am, they don't.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No ma 'am. But then everyone was poor and everybody had the same standing, so I guess maybe that's made a difference, I don't know. But I know that it's different from when I was a girl when I was young.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: So you think maybe things were more, there was more equal, things were more equal in those days than they are now. I think that's it.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah. There wasn't any society climbing.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But I wouldn't care to go back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I like my life right now. I've worked hard all my life and I could live as I want to now.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I've got a good family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When the Depression came, when the Depression came, did it give you and your husband much trouble or were you able to weather it pretty easily?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, weather it.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We had a wonderful crop that year and he went to town so this week. Came home and he said, well mommy, I sold my wheat. Sold our wheat, that's fine, 25 cents a bushel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what are we going to do with the money?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, I said the first thing we do, we'll pay our hard help. The second place we'll do, we'll pay our taxes. And then you and I and the kids will live off the land and we did. Yeah. We raised everything we ate. We didn't go to the grocery store for anything. I boarded the school teacher for 12 years out there. And she paid me $25 a month and that bought all my staples. Plus sugar and all those things like that. And we raised our own meat and eggs and cream and butter and milk and vegetables. And we had peaches in the orchard, we had apricots in the orchard, we had pears, we had apples. And we had a big strawberry patch, we had a big raspberry patch, we had a big asparagus patch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What more would we want?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That was it. If we weren't unhappy,

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: we'd live good through the Depression. Didn't have all the beautiful clothes we wanted, but I made all our clothes. So, I won't do it. Kids of today couldn't do it.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I've been told, one fellow in Troy told me that a couple of people who were kind of desperate during the Depression came into a service station. And one of them was going to do away with himself, was going to shoot himself. And he said he told him, he said he told him, you're crazy. He said, I'll get in touch with Griskell, the county commissioner. And he said, he'll fix you up and see that you get some food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember your husband having to do that as county commissioner?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I'll tell you, they used to have in Troy during the Depression, what they called a hobo camp. Down the track there, going towards Kendrick, they had a hobo camp there. And I had a crate of eggs ready to take to town. We got ten cents a dozen for them. And we used to make our own bacon, you see. And we had some bacon that was kind of fat, you know. The kids didn't like that. They liked it striped with meat. So, the hobos and the waltzes and hairy driskels had a warehouse, beans, you know, rahirates. So, Walt brought in, I said to Walt, I said, take this crate of eggs to the hobo camp. And yes, and he said, I'll get some bacon and I'll take him some bacon. And they can have bacon and eggs. And hairy driskels, his twin brother got furnished with beans. And they had a big coal oil can that they cooked those beans in over a bonfire.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And the hobos would just come running from all directions, you know, to get that food. They used to have what they call a hobo camp there in Troy. I furnished many of the crate of eggs for them. And we gave them bacon, some of them, you know, they had bacon and eggs. And hairy furnished the beans and they put bacon in with the beans and cooked them, you know. They had their protein. It sounds like they were just hand to mouth. Well, that's it. No work and no money. Somebody had to feed them. And we had a fellow that worked on our combine, our first combine. Every year he'd come to Troy and he'd be broke. My husband would give him enough money to live until the wheat was ready. And he'd come off and tend to combine.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He'd work off what your husband had given him and then he'd make some... He'd work off what your husband had given him and then he'd make some more besides. Sure, so he could live.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And then they had the CC camp, the CC boys, you know, that used to go out and plant all the trees you see planted along the highways. That was planted by the CC boys. CC camp, you know. That was during the Depression too. Gave the boys something to do. But I don't know who paid them off for that. I guess the county.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: As far as we were concerned, we didn't get very much for our wheat, but at the same time we lived good and we were happy. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever do any social life in Genesee? Did you ever go in there for entertainments or anything like that?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I used to go in for dances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And dances right in town? Dances right in town?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oddball of the fall.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I used to dance. Oh, I danced a lot in Genesee.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But I used to go with my brothers always. I had two brothers older than me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your father didn't object to dancing too much?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh no.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I used to go to a lot of dances. We used to have dances in our own home. We had this big house and we'd just roll up the rug and have French doors between the two rooms. And we used to have dances there. A lot of them in our own home. No kidding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who would play?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, there was a couple of boys, young men there in the neighborhood that would play the fiddle or the violin real well. They'd play the violin. We'd dance. Oh well, we'd go to a lot of dances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how far would people come for a dance at your house? Just neighborhood or what?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Neighbors from Cockrick and Linville, you know, in our own neighborhood. Oh, it wouldn't be too many. It would be, I imagine, 20 or 30.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah. And we had a literary society at the school always. And we'd play with the kids. All those games, skip the Malou and all of that kind of stuff, you know. Kid games. We all joined in and played with the kids. Literary society.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was part of the literary was playing games like that?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And they had a program.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they have the program first and then the games afterwards?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They'd have a program. Literary society had a program and the teacher was the president of it. And then after that we'd play games.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We'd bring lunch and have midnight lunch.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Stay late. Yeah.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, we had fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be like a potluck or how would it?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, just sometimes potluck and sometimes just sandwiches and coffee and milk for the kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would these be mostly Norwegian folks or would they be mixed? Would they be mostly just Norwegian folks or would they be Norwegians and Irish?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, they'd be just a mixture.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Mm hm. Mixture. When I started school, I couldn't speak English. All I could speak was Norwegian. You see, my grandmother couldn't speak English at all. She lived with us and I could read Norwegian and spell Norwegian. But I started, oh, I could barely speak English. I always remember how the teacher would laugh and they taught me ABC at that time. And you know when I get to H, in Norwegian it's ho. And I get to H, I say ho. Oh, now you're not driving horses. So I was getting...

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: So it took me a long time to get over that.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But the teacher boarded with us at home and she used to help me a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She would kind of give you lessons right at home?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, she didn't give me, she just helped me get started reading and all. Didn't take me long. I caught on.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, with a lot of Norwegian kids at school, you probably were tempted to speak Norwegian with them. No, we didn't, no there wasn't. There was, a lot of our neighbors were American in our neighborhood.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But on Birch Ridge, they spoke Swedish over there all the time, Swedish. And when we built our house on the farm, why, we had a Negro boy haul lumber for us. No, I'll take that back. We had a Swedish boy hauling lumber and he overtook us, a Negro boy on the road, a family by the name of Wells that lived up there. They were awfully nice people. He couldn't talk English too well and the Swedish boy couldn't. So he just started talking Swedish to him. And when he came home he said, Walt, something happened today, the funniest thing I ever, I never, I just, I can't get over it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, fine, what happened to you?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: You know, I never did know that there were Swedish niggas. He said, this man just talked Swedish just as good as I could.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I never knew before that there were Swedish niggas. Well, Walt said if you move up there you'd have to learn to talk Swedish or you'd never get along. But they taught, the kids in the playground, they'd talk Swedish all the time. And they usually had a teacher that was Swedish and they always talked Swedish in their own homes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you knew of the Wells family pretty much then?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, I didn't know the Wells family before. You knew them real well. But they were a nice family. Everybody liked them.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That's what I've heard. I've talked to old timers out of Derry. They've got a great reputation. I've never heard any of the good about them.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: You know, they're nice people.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That's a funny story. I've never heard a story like that about the Wells. But it makes sense.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd have to know how to speak Swedish, wouldn't they?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, sure. They'd have to. Well, they learned it from playing in the schoolyard.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But this boy, he was right from Sweden. His sister worked for me and she was Swedish. And he was hauling the lumber for our house. And he picked up this boy on the road and the Negro, he could see that John couldn't talk English very good in Sweden. So he just turned around and talked Swedish to him. And it scared him, I guess, at first. When he came home, he said, I run out of something today. I never was so surprised in my life. I didn't know there was Swedish niggers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you had people helping you, then hired help that were just over from the old country?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I had a girl. I had a girl by the name of Anna Lieberg and John Lieberg. They were both from Sweden, dry from Sweden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd come right out to Idaho from Sweden?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah. Pretty much. They had relatives, you know, Swedish people, like Nero. But, oh, they were wonderful people. Best workers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would that work? Would they stay bored with you for a long period of time?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They all did. They all boarded there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would they stay?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, the girls stayed three years, winter and summer.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No kidding. And we always had to hire a man the year round. The year round. And just think of $45 a month the year round. For a man. He got his room and board. He got his room and board and washing and ironing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what part of the work, for example, would she do? The girl?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: She did all the heavy work, washing and ironing and scrubbing and all that kind of work. I did the cooking usually always. She was the neatest of men. She was wonderful.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh,

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I don't know. It seems like a hundred years ago.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: It sure does seem like a very different time. Yeah. To have hired folks, hired help that was living with you year round. Now that's different than most people do today. Well, they do yet.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, usually it's not. We have a hard man out there right now. He lives there all the time and he works there. Has a bed and board. But a lot of the farmers now, they don't board them and they have to take a lunch. But I never would do that. I think when a man works hard, he's entitled to a hot meal at noon.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: That's changed, you know. But we always had a hiring man the year round.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Did you find that for these folks from Sweden that it was a little... I imagine if you came over to America and you were already grown up like these hired folks did, that it would be harder to adjust to American ways than if you came over as a little baby or a little child.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I don't think that that part, now, Hauswirth, she was doing just as much as I did about it. In fact, she was the cleanest person and the best washer and iron. She was wonderful. Of course, back there now, it's compulsory for people to learn, take English into school. I had a girl that worked right in Sweden just about four or five years ago out there on the farm and she could speak English just as good as anyone. In fact, she could speak Swedish and English and French. And she said to me after she got through working for me, she said, My, I'd like to get a job someplace. And I said, well, you're just a smart girl. I said, you go up to the University of Idaho, you might get a job up there. But they didn't have anything for her. And I said, well, you go to Pullman, Washington State University, I bet you'll get a job over there. She went to a job over there and got a job as an interpreter in language. And she taught Italian and Swedish and American, of course. And she could speak American. She graduated back in Sweden in high school. She took a head in marriage. And they told her she could enter college as a sophomore, just out of high school.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They're different back there from what they used to be, too. But she was a smart girl. She married one of our neighbor boys, lives in Seattle now. He's in public accountants there.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, I suppose I should get going. I suppose I should get going. I've stayed and talked to you for quite a while.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Well, I don't know if I'd give you any information at all, but... I think you have. Keep my name out of the paper, out of the book. I don't want anyone to think I... He was too old. He was your father. It was my husband's father that came. And I don't think Mr. Cunningham came with him, I don't know. But he went back and brought the whole outfit out here. My father -in -law. He was the oldest. And then Ingo, and then me, and then Anton, and then Ida. Ida, my sister Ida, was a graduate registered nurse. Eddie, the baby.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I'm just curious about when you were being their mother and raising them up. Some of them were probably home for quite a few years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did what?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Some of them were still at home for quite a few years.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: My two oldest brothers, though. When they were...

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I don't know how old. I've forgotten now. But they were homesteaded in Montana. In Devon, Montana. They went up there and took homesteads up there. They were gone then. And my younger brother, gone younger than me, Anton, he rented the home place and would find that. He married a girl from Genesee by the name of Burr. Mary Burr. They're all gone now. And my sister Ida was... I wanted to be a nurse so bad. The doctor wanted me to be a nurse, but I said... I said, no, you can't do this work at home. You better go. But if you go and be a nurse, you stay with it. You can't come home. You've got to graduate. And so she went training for nursing. She used to write home and say, oh, I can't take all these ballings off. Well, you take it. Other girls do. You take it and stay. Because you can't go home. Otherwise, I'll go. And you stay here and do the work. But she stayed and graduated. She was a wonderful nurse. She was a registered nurse.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: She married a fellow in Seattle. And of course, they were divorced. But she has five children. And her oldest, the grandchild, is a doctor now. And the other one is in medical school. He's going to be a doctor. So she did well.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: So you didn't try to become a nurse after that. Oh, no. I couldn't. I had to stay home.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But the doctor, the in -genesis doctor, wanted me to become a nurse because he said, you've done such a good job taking care of your own family. And he said, I want you to be a nurse. But my sister, I don't want to be. She said, no, I want to be. I think I want to be a nurse. So I said, all right. You go on. You stay with it. Otherwise, you have to come home and do what I'm doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that you had to be strict when you were mothering the kids, bringing them up?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Not too bad. No. We got along just wonderful. If they didn't behave, I'd tell my dad.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And he was strict. Yeah, you bet he was strict.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He was strict. At the same time, he was a good father.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: You know, I've been told that by quite a few people.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Their fathers were pretty strict in those days. I think in those days. In older days, they were. More so than the mothers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father being strict, did that mean that he punished the kids pretty easily?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I can't think of anyone punishing him, hitting him or anything like that.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: They knew not to mind him. And I did too.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He must have grown to depend on you a great deal when you took care of the house.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: In fact, he didn't want me to get married at all. He was selfish that way. But I took care of him after he had his stroke. I took care of him for five months before he died.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He hated losing me.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Sure, I can understand why.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: But I'm not sorry. Yeah, I did it again.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I guess that life was harder then, and you had to do what needed to be done to get by.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: He didn't live to be too old a man. He was just 64 when he died. Had a stroke and died. But he was awfully good to his mother, I tell you. She lived with us for 18 years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have timber on the place? Was it easy for you to get your wood to burn for winter?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, they used to go up to Troy and buy the cordwood every summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they cut it or buy it?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, they'd buy it already cut. The cordwood already cut. And then they'd bring it home and saw it up. They'd split it from the stove and leave some chunks to the heating stove.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: So there was really no wood in the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was no wood right handy around the vicinity?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh no, they had to go up near Troy to get cordwood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about water? Was that easy for you to get where you lived?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, we had water in the house.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: We had a well and a windmill and pumped on top of the hill. And then came back, had cold water in the house. Had a sink in the house with cold water, but not hot water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you had a windmill?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Uh huh, I had a windmill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the neighbors have windmills then too?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, take my father. He'd have windmills, you know. He was the first one to have everything. He was the first one to have a hack, first one to have a buggy, first one to have a car, first one to have a windmill. And I don't know how he did it, but he did. Never in debt. Never. He never bought anything, but he paid for it.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: I wonder how it came about that he could even afford to, you know, to buy at all. I suppose it must have been the good crops that he had, the good land. Oh, he had good crops and good prices.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But a windmill now, is that really useful to have? Did it work well?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Sure, we had a wonderful well.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: It kept the cattle trough full of water all the time. We had a cistern on top of a hill, and it piped back down to the house, and it had a sink in the house with cold water. And then he, we had a bathroom that he fixed, and fixed corals in the cook stove on top of the range. We had hot water, we had baths, bathtub. There's a big sink bathtub.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the other neighbors have, was water commonly easy to get in your neighborhood?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Oh, it was awfully easy to get water. It was. You didn't have to drop a well too deep or anything. No, I think they dug it by hand, you know. It wasn't too deep, but lots of water. Uh huh.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Yeah. Oh, it was really.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of the neighboring families, did many of them stay, or most of them stay when you were growing up? Was there, what I'm wondering is, was there much turnover of the farms?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: No, no, not, that's about the time, about the time that my father quit farming, but some of them quit farming, and moved to town. Some of them sold their place, the Rosner place was sold, the Duggar place was sold, but the Danielson's, they still stayed.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: And of course we sold our place to a fellow by the name of Berger, and he still has it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when your father passed away?

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: Twenty -eight.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: All the other kids had just about grown by then. Oh, they were all grown, all of them.

speaker: Jennie Halverson Driscoll
words: My youngest brother was, I think he must have been about twenty. Well, I will, I will get going, I can, it must be.
